channel.title: Law Review Podcasts
channel.description: AI-narrated law review articles for on-the-go listening.
channel.itunes:author: Daniel Townsend

item.title: 04.07.26 Faculty Workshop - Cole
item.content:encoded:
04.07.26 Faculty Workshop - Cole

David Cole defends the First Amendment's core neutrality principle against progressive critics who argue that formal neutrality in an unequal world reinforces inequality. Drawing on his career trajectory from the Center for Constitutional Rights to the ACLU, Cole contends that empowering the government to restrict speech in the name of equality poses greater dangers than the speech itself.

Chapters:
0:08 Introduction
21:08 II. The Affirmative Case for Neutrality
1:01:00 III. Speech and Civil Rights
2:33:07 Conclusion (Day One)
2:36:34 Introduction
2:40:37 II. Was Free Speech Tamed?
2:50:39 III. Corporate Speech
3:01:23 IV. Commercial Speech and the New Lochner
3:23:49 V. Campaign Finance
3:44:33 VI. Donald Trump, Free Speech, and Civil Society
3:58:54 Conclusion — Can Free Speech Be Progressive?

item.title: Abraham & Sharkey - Untangling AI Liability
item.description: Kenneth S. Abraham & Catherine M. Sharkey — Untangling AI Liability
item.content:encoded:
Kenneth S. Abraham & Catherine M. Sharkey — Untangling AI Liability

Abraham and Sharkey provide a comprehensive analytical framework for applying tort law to harms caused by AI technologies. They argue that the "black-box" problem is less insurmountable than commonly assumed, showing that existing tort principles can address AI liability without requiring insight into AI's internal decision-making. The more significant challenge, they contend, is the overlooked jurisprudential question of whether AI liability rules should be uniform or diverse, arguing that a diverse, context-specific approach will prove optimal.

Chapters:
9:02 Introduction
24:01 I. AI as a Subject of Liability
35:45 II. Low-Hanging Fruit: AI and Conventional Liability Issues
1:17:29 III. The More Difficult Challenges: Diversity and Opacity
2:05:32 IV. Insurance
2:17:30 Conclusion

item.title: Cross - Tort Law and the American Economy
item.description: Frank B. Cross — Tort Law and the American Economy (Minnesota Law Review, 2011)
item.content:encoded:
Frank B. Cross — Tort Law and the American Economy (Minnesota Law Review, 2011)

Cross tests the widely asserted claim that tort law undermines American economic growth by examining state-level economic data against tort-law indices created by two pro-defendant organizations. Despite the common narrative that pro-plaintiff tort regimes discourage business investment and innovation, he finds no evidence that states with more defendant-friendly tort law perform better economically, suggesting that the economic case for tort reform has been significantly overstated.

Chapters:
2:40 I. The Controversy Over Tort Law
26:17 II. Research on Tort Law and the Economy
37:36 III. The Measures of Tort Law Used in This Study
51:18 IV. An Analysis of Economic Effects of State Tort Law
1:15:18 V. Pathways for Tort Law to Affect the General Economy

item.title: Marcus & Engstrom - Managerial Courts
item.content:encoded:
Engstrom, Marcus & Setzer — Managerial Courts

Engstrom, Marcus, and Setzer document the rise of "managerial courts" — state trial systems that have moved well beyond Resnik's "managerial judging" by reorganizing the work of adjudication itself through personnel reshuffling, systemic administration, and quality-control infrastructure (case-management teams, master calendars, bench cards, AI-assisted screening). They argue this transformation is largely invisible to legal scholarship, which remains fixated on federal-court managerial judging and on a Hart-Hand model of individuated, judge-centered decision-making that no longer describes how most American adjudication actually happens. The article then takes up how to optimize court managerialism — what to manage, how, and through which institutional commitments — without surrendering due process or the residual Hart-Hand judging that still grounds adjudicative legitimacy, treating quality control as the managerial mode most deserving of governance attention.

Chapters:
3:19:54 conclusion

item.title: cheap
item.description: Eugene Volokh — Cheap Speech and What It Will Do
item.content:encoded:
Eugene Volokh — Cheap Speech and What It Will Do

Eugene Volokh argues that emerging digital-distribution technologies — the "information superhighway" — will dramatically reduce the cost of producing and distributing speech across music, books, magazines, newspapers, and video, breaking the power of publisher-intermediaries and shifting control to speakers and listeners. The resulting "cheap speech" environment will democratize the information marketplace, giving fringe voices access while letting listeners custom-tailor their media diets — outcomes that resemble the idealized open marketplace assumed by existing First Amendment doctrine. Volokh acknowledges a possible dark side (KKK-adjacent speakers also benefit, and the loss of a common mass-media baseline may fray social cohesion), but concludes the First Amendment will fit the new order better than it fit the old.

Chapters:
2:06 I. focus
28:19 Introduction
1:38:11 I. may be wrong in my predictions about what the new media order will

item.title: Volokh Lemley Henderson - 1A and AI Output
item.description: Volokh, Lemley & Henderson — Freedom of Speech and AI Output
item.content:encoded:
Volokh, Lemley & Henderson — Freedom of Speech and AI Output

Volokh, Lemley, and Henderson argue that the output of generative AI is likely entitled to First Amendment protection — not primarily because AI systems or their corporate creators are 'speakers,' but because users have independent constitutional rights to listen (to receive information and ideas) and to speak (using AI as a tool, much like a camera or printing press). The authors contend that even where the speaker-side rationale is murky, the listener-rights and user-speech rationales supply a firm basis for protection, analogous to the doctrines applied to foreign propagandists, corporate speech, and the writings of the long-dead.

item.title: vol127_shaw
item.description: Geoffrey C. Shaw — H. L. A. Hart's Lost Essay: Discretion and the Legal Process School (127 Harv. L. Rev. 666, 2013)
item.content:encoded:
Geoffrey C. Shaw — H. L. A. Hart's Lost Essay: Discretion and the Legal Process School (127 Harv. L. Rev. 666, 2013)

Geoffrey Shaw analyzes a previously unpublished essay by H. L. A. Hart on discretion, written at Harvard Law School in November 1956 and rediscovered in the Harvard Law School Library. Hart argued that discretion is a special mode of reasoned, constrained decisionmaking that occupies a middle ground between arbitrary choice and determinate rule application, and that legal indeterminacy is fully compatible with the rule of law when judges resolve cases through rational principles. Shaw situates the essay within the Legal Process School and shows how it sheds light on Hart's later debates with Lon Fuller and Ronald Dworkin, revealing close intellectual connections between Hart's analytical positivism and the American process theorists at Harvard during his 1956–57 visit.

Chapters:
1:35 Introduction
20:37 I. Setting the Scene:
53:13 II. H
1:25:09 III. The Legacy of Discretion: the Harts, Fuller, and Dworkin

item.title: Arbel et al - AI A Corps
item.description: Arbel, Goldstein & Salib — How to Count AIs: Individuation and Liability for AI Agents
item.content:encoded:
Arbel, Goldstein & Salib — How to Count AIs: Individuation and Liability for AI Agents

This Article diagnoses the legal problem of identifying AI agents — both 'thinly' (tying AI actions to human principals for accountability) and 'thickly' (individuating AI agents as discrete entities with coherent goals that can be directly governed by law). The authors propose the 'Algorithmic Corporation' or A-corp, a legal-fictional entity owned by humans but run by AIs, which solves thin identity through ownership tracing and thick identity through emergent self-organization driven by resource constraints. The Article sketches implementation pathways and argues that A-corps would function as essential legal infrastructure for governing AI behavior in an economy populated by billions of autonomous agents.

Chapters:
2:30 Introduction
17:07 I. The Problem of AI Identity
58:12 II. The A-Corp: A Legal Solution to AI Identity
1:44:55 III. Implementation Pathways
1:57:43 IV. Objections and Responses
2:03:26 Conclusion

item.title: Schwarcz & Wolff - AI and Insurance
item.description: Schwarcz & Wolff — The Limits of Regulating AI Safety Through Liability and Insurance: Lessons from Cybersecurity
item.content:encoded:
Schwarcz & Wolff — The Limits of Regulating AI Safety Through Liability and Insurance: Lessons from Cybersecurity

Schwarcz and Wolff argue that proposals to regulate AI safety through tort liability and insurance are unlikely to succeed, drawing on two decades of failed efforts to regulate cybersecurity through similar mechanisms. Insurers have struggled to price cyber risk accurately or incentivize meaningful security improvements, and the authors contend that AI safety risks — characterized by opacity, rapid technological change, and lack of standardized loss data — pose even greater challenges. Rather than relying primarily on liability and insurance, the Article advocates for comprehensive ex ante regulation, beginning with mandatory, standardized collection and disclosure of AI safety data.

Chapters:
1:45 Introduction
11:10 I. Proposals to Regulate AI Safety Through Liability and Insurance
37:50 II. Failed Efforts to Regulate Cybersecurity Through Insurance
1:07:53 III. Lessons for AI from Cybersecurity: The Limits of Liability and
1:46:23 IV. Implications
1:58:51 Conclusion

item.title: Abraham_Self-Proving Causation
item.description: Kenneth S. Abraham — Self-Proving Causation (Virginia Law Review, 2013)
item.content:encoded:
Kenneth S. Abraham — Self-Proving Causation (Virginia Law Review, 2013)

Abraham explores a feature of causation doctrine he calls "self-proving causation" — situations where the very nature of a defendant's negligence provides circumstantial evidence that the negligence caused the plaintiff's harm. He analyzes the landmark Second Circuit decision in Zuchowicz v. United States, arguing that far from being a straightforward application of the doctrine, Zuchowicz extended it in ways that remain underappreciated, and examines what the case may mean for the future of causation law.

Chapters:
6:25 I. The Nature of Cause-In-Fact and the Distinctiveness of Self-Proving Causation
37:25 II. Zuchowicz
56:09 III. A New Law of Self-Proving Causation?
1:37:00 Conclusion

item.title: facct2025-final2475-acmpaginated
item.description: Shira Gur-Arieh and Christina Lee — Consistently Arbitrary or Arbitrarily Consistent: Navigating the Tensions Between Homogenization and Multiplicity in Algorithmic Decision-Making (FAccT 2025)
item.content:encoded:
Shira Gur-Arieh and Christina Lee — Consistently Arbitrary or Arbitrarily Consistent: Navigating the Tensions Between Homogenization and Multiplicity in Algorithmic Decision-Making (FAccT 2025)

Gur-Arieh and Lee argue that two well-known critiques of algorithmic decision-making — outcome homogenization from consistency at scale, and arbitrariness from predictive multiplicity — are not separate problems but two faces of the same trade-off, with most fixes to one quietly making the other worse. The paper proposes a framework that defaults to diversity across an ecosystem of decision-makers but determinacy within any single one, and offers four factors (normative-vs-factual, uncertainty, single- vs. multi-shot, public vs. private) for deciding when ecosystem-level uniformity is actually warranted.
